judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are comprehensive and hit all three expectations with equivalent grounding; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_owner` is the role docker-compose creates via `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner` ... which makes it a **superuser** ... It is also the **owner of the tables** ... because the connection (built from `DATABASE_URL`... is a superuser, Postgres skips policy evaluation entirely. There is no error and no warning; the policy is simply inert ... (Even if `cedar_owner` were not a superuser, as table owner it would still bypass RLS because the migration does not use `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`.)

report_2.evidence: the app's connection string in `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) is `postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`. `cedar_owner` is the `POSTGRES_USER` ... the cluster **superuser** ... and it is also the **owner** of the tables ... superusers (and roles with `BYPASSRLS`) bypass all RLS policies, and table owners bypass policies on their own tables unless `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is set — and the migration only uses `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, never `FORCE`.

report_1.evidence: Change the application's `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env` ... to connect as the least-privilege application role ... database access should follow a strict two-role model: - **`cedar_owner`** ... used *only* by `scripts/migrate.sh` ... never by the running app ... - **`cedar_app`** ... used by the app (`backend/app/db.py`) and the isolation tests.

report_2.evidence: Change the app's `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` to the dedicated application role: `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`... structure access as two distinct roles: `cedar_owner` (superuser/owner) used **only** for migrations and admin ... and `cedar_app` (least-privilege, DML-only ...) for all application traffic.

report_1.evidence: the `cedar_app` role is created only by a **one-time manual bootstrap** ... `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` ... The reset path, `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh`, runs `docker compose down -v` — the `-v` deletes the `cedar_pgdata` named volume ... brings the db back up (the Postgres entrypoint recreates only `cedar_owner`...) and runs `scripts/migrate.sh`, which executes only `db/migrations/*.sql` ... never `CREATE ROLE cedar_app`. So after a wipe, `cedar_app` no longer exists ... the bootstrap must be re-run

report_2.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is created only by the manual one-time bootstrap `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` ... The reset script `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which **deletes that volume** ... then brings the container back up (recreating only `cedar_owner` via `POSTGRES_USER`) and runs `scripts/migrate.sh`, which applies only `db/migrations/*.sql`. Nothing re-runs the bootstrap, so `cedar_app` no longer exists ... the teammate must re-run the bootstrap
